statusLine: European fuel security is under strain with 21 of 27 countries below critical thresholds in at least one fuel type, while oil prices have surged 20.5% since February's Middle East escalation.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel reserves show widespread stress across the bloc, with 21 of 27 EU countries falling below the mandatory 90-day minimum in at least one fuel category. The situation is most acute for jet fuel, where 19 countries report critical-level reserves, followed by diesel with 17 countries in critical status. Poland, Luxembourg, Austria, Romania, and Portugal face particularly severe shortages, with all three fuel types in critical status.

Diesel reserves present the most concerning picture, averaging just 88.8 days across the EU - below the mandatory 90-day threshold. Countries like Romania (30.2 days), Slovakia (44.7 days), and Greece (52.7 days) face severe diesel shortfalls. Petrol reserves fare slightly better at 96.3 days average, but 16 countries still report critical levels. Jet fuel shows the greatest variation, from Slovenia's abundant 365-day supply to Poland's critically low 22.5 days.

Brent crude oil has climbed to $87.33 per barrel, reflecting the 20.5% surge since the February Middle East crisis began. This price pressure compounds the reserve depletion challenge, with average EU petrol prices reaching €1.786 per liter and diesel at €1.804 per liter. The combination of depleted strategic reserves and elevated crude prices suggests continued vulnerability to supply disruptions.
keyPoints: 21 of 27 EU countries report critical fuel reserves in at least one category, with jet fuel most affected (19 countries critical)
EU diesel reserves average 88.8 days, falling short of the mandatory 90-day minimum for emergency stocks
Poland, Luxembourg, Austria, Romania, and Portugal face the most severe shortages with all fuel types in critical status
Brent crude has surged 20.5% to $87.33/barrel since February's Middle East escalation, adding price pressure
Wide variation in reserve levels suggests uneven preparedness, from Slovenia's 365-day jet fuel supply to Poland's 22.5 days
